articles.title: Shakira Plays Free Concert for 2 Million on Copacabana Beach
articles.summary: Shakira performed a free show on Copacabana beach in Rio de Janeiro on Saturday night, drawing a crowd of 2 million people. Rio Mayor Eduardo Cavaliere confirmed the staggering attendance figure on X. It puts Shakira in the same league as the Beatles and the Rolling Stones, who have held some of the only other concerts to hit that scale on the same iconic stretch of sand.
articles.content:
Shakira took over Copacabana beach in Rio de Janeiro on Saturday night and absolutely broke the internet in the process. Two million people packed the famous shoreline to watch her perform for free, making it one of the biggest concerts the world has ever seen. Rio Mayor Eduardo Cavaliere posted the official attendance figure on X shortly after the show wrapped.

To put that number in perspective, 2 million people is roughly the entire population of Houston, Texas, all standing on one beach at the same time. Copacabana has hosted massive free shows before. Lady Gaga played there in 2024 in front of a crowd estimated at 2.1 million. Before that, the Rolling Stones drew about 1.5 million in 2006. Shakira just cemented herself in that ultra-rare company.

Shakira has been in a serious comeback era. After a very public split from footballer Gerard Piqué in 2022 and a high-profile tax fraud case in Spain that was settled in 2023, she channelled everything into her music and her image. The result has been a string of massive global hits and a touring run that has kept her at the top of the conversation for the better part of two years.

A free beach concert of this scale is the kind of cultural moment that transcends music. It is a statement. Two million people do not show up by accident. They show up because an artist has earned it, and right now Shakira has clearly earned it.
keyPoints: 2 million people attended Shakira's free concert on Copacabana beach in Rio
Rio Mayor Eduardo Cavaliere confirmed the attendance figure on X
The crowd puts Shakira alongside Lady Gaga and the Rolling Stones in Copacabana history
The show is part of Shakira's major comeback following her split from Gerard Piqué and a Spanish tax case

articles.title: Oscars Ban AI Actors and Scripts, Hitting Tilly Norwood Directly
articles.summary: The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences has ruled that AI-generated actors and AI-written scripts are now ineligible for Oscar consideration. The rule change is a direct blow to Tilly Norwood, an AI-generated actress whose work had been generating awards buzz. This is one of the most significant lines the film industry has drawn in response to the rise of AI in Hollywood.
articles.content:
The Oscars just drew a hard line. The Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences has confirmed that AI-generated actors and AI-written scripts are no longer eligible for Oscar awards. If a performance was generated by artificial intelligence rather than delivered by a human being, it cannot be nominated. If a script was written by AI rather than a human writer, same result. The rule is now official.

The announcement lands as bad news specifically for Tilly Norwood, an AI-generated actress who had been building real momentum in Hollywood conversations. Tilly Norwood is not a human performer. She is a fully artificial creation, and her work had reached a level of quality and visibility that made awards eligibility a genuine question worth asking. The Academy has now answered that question clearly: no.

For context, the Oscars are awarded by the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences and are the film industry's most prestigious honours. A nomination or win can transform the commercial and cultural trajectory of a film. The Academy making an explicit ruling about AI is significant because it signals that the institution sees AI-generated performance and writing as a fundamentally different category from human creative work, not just a tool but a replacement that changes what the work actually is.

The decision reflects a broader tension that has been building in Hollywood since AI tools became powerful enough to generate convincing performances and coherent scripts. Writers and actors have both fought hard against AI encroachment. The Writers Guild of America strike in 2023 included major battles over AI use in scriptwriting. SAG-AFTRA, the actors union, has also pushed back hard on AI-generated likenesses and performances. The Academy's ruling aligns the Oscars with the human side of that fight.

What happens next for Tilly Norwood and other AI-generated performers is genuinely uncertain. They can still appear in films. Films featuring them can still be released and make money. But the most prestigious stage in cinema will not be open to them, at least not under the current rules. Whether that changes as AI becomes even more embedded in production is a question the industry will be wrestling with for years.
keyPoints: The Oscars have ruled AI-generated actors and AI-written scripts ineligible for nomination
Tilly Norwood, an AI-generated actress with awards buzz, is directly affected by the new rule
The Academy sees AI-generated work as a different category from human creative work
Hollywood unions including SAG-AFTRA and the Writers Guild have long pushed back against AI use
AI films and performers can still be released commercially but will not qualify for Oscar consideration

articles.title: US-Iran War Has Killed Dubai Tourism, Costing £500M Daily
articles.summary: Dubai's tourism industry has collapsed since the United States attacked Iran in February 2026, with Iranian retaliatory missile and drone strikes targeting the city scaring away almost all foreign visitors. The city is now losing close to £500 million every single day. The people suffering most are the millions of migrant workers who built Dubai and now find themselves jobless, broke, and unable to afford a flight home.
articles.content:
Dubai used to be one of the most visited cities on the planet. Gleaming towers, luxury hotels, year-round sun and a reputation as the ultimate layover destination. But since the United States launched an attack on Iran in February 2026, all of that has effectively stopped. Iran responded with missiles and drones, and Dubai became a target. That was enough to empty the airports.

The numbers are brutal. Dubai is estimated to be losing close to £500 million every day as tourists stay away. Hotels that were booked solid are now near-empty. Malls that buzzed with shoppers from every corner of the world are quiet. The city built its entire identity around being a global crossroads, and right now that identity is in freefall.

But the headline economic figure is not even the most urgent part of the story. Dubai runs on migrant labour. Around 90 percent of its population are foreign nationals, mostly from South Asia and Southeast Asia, who came to work in construction, hospitality, retail and domestic services. These workers lived pay-cheque to pay-cheque at the best of times. Now that the work has dried up, many cannot afford the plane ticket to go home. They are stranded in a city that no longer needs them, without income and without an easy exit.

This crisis puts a very human face on what can otherwise sound like an abstract geopolitical conflict. The US-Iran war did not just rattle stock markets or disrupt oil prices. It has left hundreds of thousands of ordinary people in genuine despair in a city that sold itself to the world as a dream destination. Whether Dubai can recover depends entirely on how long the conflict lasts and whether the threat of Iranian strikes fades. Right now, neither of those things looks certain.
keyPoints: Dubai's tourism industry has collapsed since the US attacked Iran in February 2026
Iranian missile and drone strikes on Dubai have scared off almost all foreign visitors
The city is losing roughly £500 million every day in lost tourism revenue
Millions of migrant workers are now jobless and cannot afford to fly home
Around 90 percent of Dubai's population are foreign nationals dependent on the city's economy

articles.title: Golden Tempo Wins Kentucky Derby, Trainer DeVaux Makes History
articles.summary: Golden Tempo stormed from the back of the field to win the 2026 Kentucky Derby, and trainer Cherie DeVaux became the first woman ever to train a Kentucky Derby winner. The Kentucky Derby is horse racing's most prestigious American race, held annually at Churchill Downs in Louisville and nicknamed the most exciting two minutes in sports. DeVaux's win is one of the most significant milestones in the sport's history.
articles.content:
Horse racing just made history. Golden Tempo came from dead last in the field to win a thrilling 2026 Kentucky Derby on Saturday, and trainer Cherie DeVaux became the first woman to ever win the race as a trainer. The Kentucky Derby has been running since 1875, so that is 151 years before a woman broke through in the training role.

The race itself was a genuine drama. Golden Tempo was not leading heading into the final stretch. The horse came from the back of the pack, picking off rivals one by one before surging to the front at the crucial moment. That kind of come-from-behind win is exactly what makes the Derby appointment viewing every single year.

DeVaux's achievement matters beyond the sport. There have been female jockeys in the Derby before, and women have been part of horse racing at every level for decades. But the trainer is the person who shapes everything. They decide the prep races, the training regimen, the race-day strategy. Winning the Kentucky Derby as a trainer is one of the most respected accomplishments in all of American sport. DeVaux now owns that record outright.

The story of Golden Tempo and Cherie DeVaux is the kind of thing that will be replayed in highlight packages for decades. First-time milestones at historic events do not come around often. This one will stick.
keyPoints: Golden Tempo won the 2026 Kentucky Derby after coming from the back of the field
Trainer Cherie DeVaux became the first woman ever to train a Kentucky Derby winner
The Kentucky Derby has been running since 1875, making this a 151-year milestone
The come-from-behind victory made the race itself a thriller on top of the historic result

articles.title: Aziz Ansari Plays Kash Patel on SNL With a Killer Self-Aware Punchline
articles.summary: Aziz Ansari guest-starred on Saturday Night Live to play FBI Director Kash Patel in the show's cold open on 3 May 2026, delivering the night's sharpest line: 'I'm the first Indian person to suck at their job.' The bit slots Ansari into SNL's ongoing tradition of celebrities playing Trump administration officials for political satire. It is already one of the most quoted SNL moments of the season.
articles.content:
Aziz Ansari dropped into Saturday Night Live's cold open last night to play Kash Patel, the real-life FBI Director appointed under the Trump administration. If you are not familiar with Patel, he is a former Trump aide whose confirmation as FBI Director was controversial and closely watched. SNL has been running a full cast of celebrity impersonators covering the Trump administration, and Ansari joining that lineup felt like a natural and very funny addition.

The line that is already everywhere this morning came straight from Ansari's Patel: 'I'm the first Indian person to suck at their job.' It is a sharp, self-aware joke that works on multiple levels. Ansari is using his own identity to turn the usual 'representation wins' narrative on its head, and the delivery landed hard in the SNL studio.

Ansari is best known for creating and starring in the Netflix series 'Master of None', which won him Emmy Awards and made him one of the most respected voices in comedy. Seeing him step into political sketch territory on SNL felt like a reminder that he can do just about anything in a comedy context. The cold open format on SNL is famously high-pressure and high-visibility, and Ansari made it look easy.

SNL's tradition of celebrities playing political figures goes back decades, from Chevy Chase's Gerald Ford to Alec Baldwin's long run as Donald Trump. Ansari as Patel fits right into that lineage. The fact that a joke about being the first Indian person to underperform in a powerful role is the one that cuts through says a lot about where political comedy is right now.

This is the kind of SNL moment that reminds you why the show still matters. One guest, one killer line, and the whole internet is quoting it by Sunday morning.
keyPoints: Aziz Ansari played FBI Director Kash Patel in SNL's cold open on 3 May 2026
His standout line was 'I'm the first Indian person to suck at their job'
Kash Patel is the real-life FBI Director whose appointment under Trump was widely debated
Ansari is best known for the Netflix series 'Master of None'
SNL has been casting celebrities as Trump administration officials throughout the current political season

articles.title: Jule Brand's 87th-Minute Goal Knocks Arsenal Out of Women's Champions League
articles.summary: Lyon knocked Arsenal out of the UEFA Women's Champions League semi-finals on Saturday with a 4-3 aggregate win, sealed by Jule Brand's dramatic goal in the 87th minute. Arsenal had been defending the title they won last season, making the defeat especially painful. Lyon will now go on to the final, extending their status as the most dominant club in the history of the Women's Champions League.
articles.content:
It was the kind of ending that makes football genuinely cruel. Arsenal went into their Women's Champions League semi-final second leg needing a result to defend the title they won last season, and for most of the match it looked like they might just pull it off. Then Jule Brand happened. The Germany international scored in the 87th minute to give Lyon a 4-3 win on aggregate and end Arsenal's dreams of back-to-back European titles.

For context, the UEFA Women's Champions League is the top club competition in European women's football, the equivalent of the men's Champions League. Arsenal won it for the first time in the 2024-25 season, which made them the reigning champions heading into this campaign. Getting knocked out at the semi-final stage by Lyon, who are effectively the most successful club in the competition's history, still stings massively for the club and its fans.

Lyon are not just a good team. They are a dynasty. The French club have won the Women's Champions League a record number of times and have been in the final more often than any other club in the competition's history. Getting past them at this stage of the tournament is one of the hardest asks in club football. Arsenal gave them a genuine fight across both legs, which is something to hold onto, but the result is what it is.

Jule Brand has been one of the standout players in women's club football this season and her late winner was a reminder of exactly why Lyon spent the money to bring her in. For Arsenal, the focus now shifts to what they can salvage domestically, and how they rebuild to take another run at Europe next season. Lyon move forward to the Women's Champions League final, where they will be heavy favourites to add another title to that already enormous trophy cabinet.
keyPoints: Lyon beat Arsenal 4-3 on aggregate in the Women's Champions League semi-final
Jule Brand scored the winning goal in the 87th minute to seal the tie
Arsenal had been defending the Women's Champions League title they won last season
Lyon are the most decorated club in Women's Champions League history
Lyon now advance to the final as heavy favourites
articles.wikiSearchQuery: UEFA Women's Champions League 2025-26 semi-final Lyon Arsenal

articles.title: Ralph Fiennes, Colin Farrell and Wagner Moura Star in Fernando Meirelles Comedy Art
articles.summary: Ralph Fiennes, Colin Farrell and Wagner Moura are all set to star in Art, a film adaptation of the hit stage play directed by Fernando Meirelles and written by Oscar-winning screenwriter Christopher Hampton. The project is being launched at the Cannes Market by sales company 193. Putting the director of City of God and The Two Popes together with this cast for a comedy is one of the most intriguing combinations announced so far this year.
articles.content:
Art is coming to the big screen with a genuinely remarkable team behind it. Fernando Meirelles, the Brazilian director best known for City of God and The Two Popes, is directing. Christopher Hampton, who won the Academy Award for Best Adapted Screenplay for Dangerous Liaisons back in 1989, is writing the script. And the cast includes Ralph Fiennes, Colin Farrell and Wagner Moura. That is a lot of talent in one room.

Art the play has a long and celebrated history. Written by French playwright Yasmina Reza, it originally premiered in Paris in 1994 before becoming a global hit translated into dozens of languages. The story centres on three male friends whose relationships are torn apart by a dispute over a painting, specifically one friend's purchase of an expensive all-white canvas. It sounds simple, but it cuts deep into questions about taste, ego, friendship and what we owe each other. The combination of sharp comedy and genuine emotional pain made it a stage phenomenon.

Christopher Hampton is actually the person who translated Reza's original French text into English for the stage production, so his involvement in the screenplay feels like a natural continuation. He knows this material better than almost anyone. Hampton's other credits include the screenplay for Atonement and the stage adaptation of Sunset Boulevard, so he is no stranger to prestige adaptations.

The three leads are a fascinating combination. Ralph Fiennes has spent recent years reminding everyone he is one of the best actors alive, most recently through The Menu and a long run as M in the Bond franchise. Colin Farrell is riding high off his Oscar nomination for The Banshees of Inisherin. Wagner Moura, the Brazilian actor best known internationally for playing Pablo Escobar in Narcos, brings a different energy to the group entirely. These three playing bickering friends is an extremely exciting prospect.

Sales company 193 is launching Art at the Cannes Market, which runs alongside the Cannes Film Festival each May. The Cannes Market is where distributors from around the world come to buy films for their territories, so a launch there with this cast attached should generate serious interest very quickly. No release date has been announced yet, but with Meirelles directing and this lineup confirmed, Art has just become one of the most anticipated projects on the horizon.
keyPoints: Ralph Fiennes, Colin Farrell and Wagner Moura are all cast in the film adaptation of Art
Fernando Meirelles, director of City of God and The Two Popes, is directing
Christopher Hampton, Oscar winner for Dangerous Liaisons, wrote the screenplay
Art is based on Yasmina Reza's acclaimed 1994 French play about friendship and a disputed painting
Sales company 193 is launching the project at the Cannes Market
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Art play Yasmina Reza film adaptation

articles.title: Lindsey Hall Read Her Boyfriend's ChatGPT and It Ended Their Relationship
articles.summary: Lindsey Hall discovered a conversation her boyfriend had with ChatGPT that contained harsh and hurtful thoughts about her, and the discovery ended their relationship. ChatGPT, OpenAI's AI chatbot, is often used as a private journaling or venting tool, which means people write things there they would never say out loud. Lindsey Hall's story has gone viral because it raises a question millions of couples are now quietly sitting with: is reading your partner's ChatGPT history the new reading their diary?
articles.content:
Lindsey Hall did not go looking for a fight. She came across her boyfriend's ChatGPT conversation history and what she found there ended the relationship. The conversations contained harsh thoughts he had about her, things he apparently felt comfortable saying to an AI chatbot but never to her face. Hall has spoken publicly about the experience and her story has resonated with a huge number of people who are now realising the same risk exists in their own relationships.

ChatGPT, built by OpenAI and launched in late 2022, has quietly become one of the most intimate spaces on the internet. People use it to vent, to process emotions, to say things they are too afraid or too proud to say to another person. It functions like a private diary that talks back. The problem is that unlike a diary, ChatGPT stores conversation history that can be accessed by anyone with access to the device or the account.

What makes Lindsey Hall's story hit differently is that her boyfriend was not texting another person or hiding a secret social media account. He was talking to a machine. But the thoughts were still his. The harshness was still real. And the discovery still broke something that could not be unbroken. Hall's account forces a genuinely uncomfortable question: if your partner writes something cruel about you to an AI, does that count as a betrayal?

There is no consensus answer yet. Some people reading Hall's story online have sided with her completely. Others have argued that private venting, even harsh venting, is a necessary emotional release and that ChatGPT is just a modern version of writing in a journal. But the journal was never one password away from being read by the person you were writing about.

This story lands in 2026 at a moment when AI tools are fully embedded in daily life for millions of people. OpenAI's ChatGPT has hundreds of millions of users worldwide. A significant number of those users are in relationships. The question of what counts as private when you are venting to an AI is not going away. Lindsey Hall's experience may be the most public version of a conversation that is already happening in private homes everywhere.
keyPoints: Lindsey Hall found harsh thoughts about herself in her boyfriend's ChatGPT conversation history
The discovery ended their relationship
ChatGPT is widely used as a private venting or journaling tool, creating new relationship risks
Unlike a traditional diary, ChatGPT history is stored digitally and can be accessed by others
The story raises a new unresolved question about privacy and emotional honesty in relationships

articles.title: Olivia Rodrigo Debuts New Track 'begged' on SNL Hosting Debut
articles.summary: Olivia Rodrigo performed a brand new unreleased song called 'begged' during her Saturday Night Live hosting debut on 3 May 2026. The track was introduced by Connor Storrie and comes from Rodrigo's forthcoming album, giving fans their first taste of what is next. It is the first time Rodrigo has hosted SNL, having previously appeared only as a musical guest.
articles.content:
Olivia Rodrigo had quite a Saturday night. She hosted Saturday Night Live for the very first time and used the moment to drop something genuinely new. The song is called 'begged' and it is an unreleased track from her upcoming album. Connor Storrie introduced the performance, and the audience got what amounted to an exclusive first listen.

Rodrigo first broke through in 2021 with her debut album Sour, which produced massive hits including 'drivers license' and 'good 4 u'. Her second album, Guts, came out in 2023 and debuted at number one in multiple countries. She has built a reputation for emotionally raw songwriting aimed squarely at the experience of being young and heartbroken, and she does it better than almost anyone working right now.

Performing new material on SNL is a classic power move. The show reaches millions of viewers live and even more online in the days after. Dropping 'begged' here rather than through a traditional single rollout means Rodrigo controls the conversation heading into whatever her next album campaign looks like. It creates immediate buzz and puts the new music in front of the biggest possible audience all at once.

There is no release date yet for the new album, but 'begged' is the clearest signal yet that Rodrigo is ready to move. Given how well both Sour and Guts performed, expectations are already high. After tonight, they just got higher.
keyPoints: Olivia Rodrigo hosted Saturday Night Live for the first time on 3 May 2026
She performed a new unreleased song called 'begged' during the show
Connor Storrie introduced Rodrigo's performance of 'begged'
The track comes from Rodrigo's forthcoming third album
Rodrigo previously released Sour in 2021 and Guts in 2023, both to huge commercial success

articles.title: Chinese Court Rules AI Cannot Legally Replace a Human Worker
articles.summary: A Chinese court has ruled that a company cannot fire a worker simply to replace them with an AI system, setting a landmark legal precedent for workers' rights in the age of automation. The ruling included a pointed line: "Technological progress may be irreversible, but it cannot exist outside a legal framework." It is one of the first rulings anywhere in the world to directly protect a worker from AI-driven job displacement through the courts.
articles.content:
A court in China just drew a line in the sand on AI and employment. The ruling is straightforward: a company cannot legally fire a worker for the purpose of replacing that person with an AI system. The case is being watched closely around the world because courts in most countries have not yet dealt directly with this question, and China just answered it.

The language in the ruling is striking. The court wrote that "technological progress may be irreversible, but it cannot exist outside a legal framework." That sentence is doing a lot of work. It is not anti-technology. It is not saying AI is bad. It is saying that no matter how powerful or cost-effective AI becomes, companies still have to follow employment law. Workers still have rights. You cannot just swap out a human for a machine and call it a day.

This matters because the fear of AI replacing jobs is not theoretical anymore. It is happening right now across industries from customer service to coding to content creation. Most workers have had very little legal protection against it. Governments have talked about regulation, but actual court rulings that back workers in specific cases are rare. This one sets a precedent that other courts and lawmakers could point to.

China is an interesting place for this ruling to come from. The country has invested heavily in AI development and automation across its manufacturing and tech sectors. The fact that a Chinese court is drawing this boundary signals that even in economies racing hard toward AI adoption, there are limits that the legal system is willing to enforce.

The bigger question now is whether this ruling influences policy elsewhere. Labour lawyers in Europe, the United States, and elsewhere will be paying close attention. One court ruling does not change the world overnight, but it does give workers and their advocates a concrete example to point to the next time an employer reaches for AI as a reason to let someone go.
keyPoints: A Chinese court ruled that companies cannot legally fire workers in order to replace them with AI
The court stated that technological progress cannot exist outside a legal framework
It is one of the first court rulings anywhere to directly protect workers from AI-driven job replacement
The ruling could influence labour law debates in other countries
China itself is a major investor in AI, making this ruling a significant internal signal

articles.title: NASA Satellite Can Now Watch Mexico City Sink From Space
articles.summary: A NASA satellite is capturing real-time measurements of Mexico City sinking into the ground, and the footage is visible from space. The city is built on a drained lakebed and has been sinking for decades, but NASA's satellite now makes the scale of the problem impossible to ignore. Some parts of Mexico City are dropping by as much as 50 centimetres per year.
articles.content:
Mexico City has a sinking problem, and now the whole world can watch it happen from orbit. NASA has deployed a powerful satellite capable of measuring underground activity across large urban areas, and Mexico City is one of the most dramatic examples it has captured. The subsidence, which is the technical word for land sinking, is now visible from space in a way that makes the scale of it genuinely staggering.

The city sits on top of what used to be Lake Texcoco, which the Spanish drained centuries ago when they built on top of the Aztec city of Tenochtitlan. The problem is that the soft, water-saturated clay underneath Mexico City compresses as groundwater is pumped out to supply the city's 22 million residents. Pull the water out, and the ground above it slowly collapses. The city has been dealing with this for generations, but the NASA satellite data shows the current rate is alarming in certain districts.

NASA's satellite uses a technique called Interferometric Synthetic Aperture Radar, or InSAR, which bounces radar signals off the Earth's surface and detects tiny changes in elevation over time. It can measure movement down to the millimetre. When applied to Mexico City, the results show uneven sinking across the metropolitan area, with some zones dropping dramatically faster than others. That unevenness is actually one of the biggest engineering dangers because it puts enormous stress on buildings, roads, and water infrastructure.

This story matters beyond Mexico City. Dozens of major cities around the world, including Jakarta, Bangkok, and Shanghai, face similar sinking problems linked to groundwater extraction and urban development. NASA's satellite capability means scientists and city planners can now monitor these changes continuously and from a distance, giving them better data to work with before a slow-moving crisis becomes a fast-moving disaster.
keyPoints: NASA's satellite uses InSAR radar to measure Mexico City's sinking down to the millimetre
Mexico City is built on drained lakebed clay that compresses as groundwater is pumped out
Some areas of Mexico City are sinking by up to 50 centimetres per year
Uneven sinking across the city creates serious stress on buildings and infrastructure
Many other major cities including Jakarta and Bangkok face the same problem
articles.wikiSearchQuery: Mexico City land subsidence sinking groundwater

articles.title: TV Stars From The Pitt and The Bear Are Taking Over Broadway
articles.summary: Audiences are packing Broadway theatres right now because their favourite TV faces have shown up on stage. Patrick Ball from The Pitt, Jon Bernthal and Ayo Edebiri from The Bear, and Ben Ahlers from The Gilded Age are all performing live this season. The crossover is turning a traditionally tough ticket-selling period into a genuine cultural moment.
articles.content:
Something interesting is happening on Broadway right now. The people you have been watching on your TV and streaming queue have decided to show up in person, and audiences are going absolutely wild for it.

Patrick Ball, who plays a doctor navigating a chaotic emergency room in The Pitt, is currently on stage. Jon Bernthal and Ayo Edebiri, the co-stars of The Bear (the intense kitchen drama that has won a pile of Emmy Awards since it launched in 2022), are both performing in separate Broadway productions this season. Ben Ahlers from The Gilded Age, the HBO period drama about New York's ruthlessly wealthy elite in the 1880s, rounds out the wave of small-screen talent making the jump to live theatre.

This is not entirely new territory. TV stars have always drifted toward Broadway between seasons. But the scale of it right now feels different. These are not C-list cameos. Bernthal, Edebiri, and Ball are at the absolute peak of their cultural moment. Shows like The Bear and The Pitt have become appointment viewing in a way that few dramas manage, and their fans are motivated enough to buy theatre tickets and sit in the dark together.

For Broadway, this is genuinely good news. Live theatre has spent years clawing back audiences lost during the pandemic shutdowns of 2020 and 2021. Star power from prestige TV is one of the most reliable ways to fill seats, especially for shows that might otherwise struggle to cut through the noise. The crossover also works the other way. Stage performances tend to generate fresh buzz for actors, reminding people why they fell in love with them in the first place.

The bigger cultural signal here is about prestige and where actors want to be seen. A generation ago, film was the goal. Then prestige TV became the destination. Now Broadway is back in the conversation as the place you go to prove you are genuinely serious about the craft. If the spring 2026 season is any guide, that conversation is only getting louder.
keyPoints: Patrick Ball from The Pitt, Jon Bernthal and Ayo Edebiri from The Bear, and Ben Ahlers from The Gilded Age are all on Broadway right now
The crossover is driving strong audience turnout during what is typically a competitive season for theatre
Broadway has been rebuilding its audience since pandemic-era closures in 2020 and 2021
Prestige TV stars doing live theatre is becoming a reliable cultural trend, not just a one-off
The moment signals that Broadway is back as a serious destination for actors at the top of their game

articles.title: A Deepfake Warren Buffett Asked the First Question at Berkshire Without Buffett
articles.summary: The first Berkshire Hathaway shareholder meeting without Warren Buffett as CEO was opened by a deepfake video of Buffett himself asking new CEO Greg Abel the very first question. A familiar gravelly voice said 'Hi, my name is Warren, from Omaha' before the room realised what was happening. It was a surreal moment that blended tribute, humour, and a genuine reminder of how convincing AI-generated video has become.
articles.content:
Warren Buffett spent decades running Berkshire Hathaway's annual shareholder meeting in Omaha, Nebraska. Known as the Woodstock of Capitalism, the event draws tens of thousands of investors every year to hear Buffett speak. This year was different. Buffett, now 95, stepped down as CEO and handed the reins to Greg Abel. So when Abel opened the floor for questions, nobody quite expected what came next.

A video began to play. A gravelly, familiar voice said 'Hi, my name is Warren, from Omaha.' The voice sounded exactly like Buffett. The face looked exactly like Buffett. And then it became clear: it was a deepfake, an AI-generated video designed to look and sound like the real Warren Buffett asking the first question of the first post-Buffett era at Berkshire Hathaway.

Deepfakes are AI-created videos that map a real person's face and voice onto fabricated footage. They have been used for everything from movie de-ageing to political misinformation. Seeing one open a major corporate event is a new frontier entirely. The moment landed somewhere between a touching tribute and a genuinely unsettling demonstration of how realistic this technology has become.

For the investing world, the meeting itself was already historic. Buffett built Berkshire Hathaway into one of the most valuable companies on earth over more than five decades. Greg Abel stepping into that role is a genuine passing of the torch. The fact that the first question came from a digital ghost of Buffett says something about the moment we are all living in right now. AI is not just in our phones and laptops. It is showing up at the most human of rituals too.
keyPoints: The first Berkshire Hathaway meeting without Warren Buffett as CEO opened with a deepfake video of Buffett
The deepfake said 'Hi, my name is Warren, from Omaha' in Buffett's recognisable gravelly voice
New CEO Greg Abel was the one opening the floor when the video played
Buffett, aged 95, recently handed leadership of Berkshire Hathaway to Greg Abel
The moment highlighted how convincing and accessible AI deepfake technology has become

articles.title: Zendaya Skips Met Gala Bankrolled by Jeff Bezos
articles.summary: Zendaya is reportedly sitting out Monday's Met Gala, which is being funded this year by Amazon founder Jeff Bezos and his wife Lauren Sanchez. The snub comes after Meryl Streep also pulled out as a co-chair, adding more high-profile drama to the event. Bezos and Sanchez have been publicly aligned with Donald Trump, which appears to be a factor in the star-powered boycott.
articles.content:
The Met Gala is one of the biggest nights in fashion every year. It happens at the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York and is basically the Super Bowl of celebrity style. But this year, the guest list drama is threatening to overshadow the red carpet itself.

Zendaya is reportedly not showing up to Monday night's Met Gala, and the reason seems to come down to who is writing the cheques. Jeff Bezos, the founder of Amazon, and his wife Lauren Sanchez are bankrolling this year's event. Both Bezos and Sanchez have been publicly close to Donald Trump in recent months, which has put some celebrities in an uncomfortable spot.

Zendaya is not the only big name stepping back. Meryl Streep, who was originally signed on as a co-chair for the evening, has also pulled out. Co-chairs are usually the most prominent figures attached to the Gala each year, so Streep's exit was already a signal that something was off. Zendaya skipping entirely turns it into a full-blown storyline.

Zendaya has been one of the most talked-about Met Gala presences in recent years, known for jaw-dropping looks that go viral instantly. Her absence will be felt. The bigger question now is whether more celebrities quietly follow suit, and whether this marks a shift in how stars navigate events tied to political controversy.
keyPoints: Zendaya is reportedly skipping the 2026 Met Gala
Jeff Bezos and Lauren Sanchez are funding the event this year
Bezos and Sanchez have been publicly close to Donald Trump
Meryl Streep also withdrew as a co-chair before the event
Zendaya's absence marks a rare high-profile snub of the annual fashion event

articles.title: Antonelli Grabs Miami Pole as Verstappen Storms Back
articles.summary: Kimi Antonelli put in a stunning lap to claim pole position for the Miami Grand Prix on Saturday, with Max Verstappen qualifying in second for a resurgent Red Bull. The result is a fresh twist in a Formula 1 season that keeps refusing to settle into a clear pattern. Antonelli, still only 18, is announcing himself as a genuine title contender lap by lap.
articles.content:
Kimi Antonelli took pole position for the Miami Grand Prix on Saturday with a lap that silenced any remaining doubters. The teenager, who stepped into Lewis Hamilton's Mercedes seat for the 2025 season after Hamilton moved to Ferrari, has been building toward a moment like this. In Miami he delivered it, beating Max Verstappen to the top spot and sending a clear message to the rest of the grid.

Max Verstappen's second place is just as big a story. Red Bull had a difficult start to the 2026 season and Verstappen looked, for a rare moment in his career, like a driver without the fastest car under him. Miami suggests that is changing. A front-row lockout for two of the sport's most exciting talents makes Sunday's race appointment viewing.

Formula 1 returns to Miami at a circuit that has become one of the sport's glitziest stops since it joined the calendar in 2022. The race weekend wraps around Hard Rock Stadium and draws a crowd that is as interested in the celebrity guest list as the lap times. But on Saturday the sport itself provided all the drama needed.

The 2026 season has been unusually unpredictable with new technical regulations reshuffling the order at nearly every round. That unpredictability is great for fans and painful for teams trying to find consistency. Antonelli on pole and Verstappen right behind him sets up a race where the winner is genuinely hard to call, which is exactly what Formula 1 wants Miami to look like.
keyPoints: Kimi Antonelli claimed pole position for the 2026 Miami Grand Prix
Max Verstappen qualified second, signalling a Red Bull comeback
Antonelli races for Mercedes after taking Lewis Hamilton's seat in 2025
New 2026 technical regulations have made the season unusually unpredictable
The Miami Grand Prix has been on the F1 calendar since 2022

articles.title: Inaudible Sound Waves From Old Buildings May Cause Ghost Sightings
articles.summary: New research suggests that infrasound, low-frequency sound waves produced by old buildings, may be the real reason people report feeling ghostly presences. The waves are inaudible to human ears but can cause feelings of unease, dread, and the sense that something is in the room with you. It is one of the most compelling scientific explanations yet for one of humanity's oldest mysteries.
articles.content:
Scientists may have finally cracked one of the most enduring mysteries in human history. New research published in May 2026 suggests that infrasound, sound waves pitched too low for human ears to consciously detect, could be responsible for the eerie feelings people associate with ghost sightings and haunted places.

Infrasound sits below 20 Hz, which is the lower limit of normal human hearing. You cannot hear it, but your body can feel it. At certain frequencies, infrasound has been shown to cause anxiety, a creeping sense of dread, blurred vision, and crucially, the overwhelming feeling that another presence is nearby. Old buildings are particularly good at generating these waves because of their large cavities, aging pipes, and the way wind moves through their structures.

The theory is not entirely new. Researchers have floated the infrasound idea for years, but this latest work strengthens the case significantly. Scientist Vic Tandy first made the connection back in the late 1990s after he noticed his own lab felt deeply unsettling and discovered a standing wave at 18.98 Hz was filling the room. The new research builds on that foundation with broader evidence from multiple building types and environments.

So what does this mean for every ghost story you have ever heard? It means that the haunted mansion, the creepy old hospital, and the Victorian house at the end of the street may genuinely make you feel something real, just not for supernatural reasons. The building itself could be playing tricks on your nervous system without you ever knowing it.
keyPoints: Infrasound waves below 20 Hz are inaudible but can make people feel anxious, uneasy, or watched
Old buildings naturally produce these waves through their structure, pipes, and cavities
Researcher Vic Tandy first linked infrasound to ghost feelings in the late 1990s
The new research broadens that evidence across multiple building types
This does not mean your feelings are imaginary — the physical effect on your body is real

articles.title: Ukraine Is Training Real Drone Pilots Inside GTA V
articles.summary: Ukraine's defense ministry has confirmed that Grand Theft Auto V is being used to help train drone pilots. The game does not replace hands-on field training, but officials say it is a useful and accessible tool. A spokesperson described it as 'a great way to relax' while building spatial awareness and flight instincts.
articles.content:
Ukraine's defense ministry has confirmed something that sounds like a joke but is completely real. Drone pilots in Ukraine are using Grand Theft Auto V as part of their training routine. The open-world crime game, originally released by Rockstar Games in 2013, lets players fly helicopters, planes, and other vehicles across a massive simulated city and landscape. It turns out those mechanics are more useful than most people would have guessed.

The ministry was clear that Grand Theft Auto V does not replace actual drone training. Real field work, proper simulators, and hands-on practice still make up the core of how Ukraine prepares its pilots. But the game fills a specific gap. It is cheap, widely available, and lets pilots practice spatial reasoning and vehicle control in a low-stakes environment whenever they have downtime.

A spokesperson for Ukraine's defense ministry put it plainly, calling Grand Theft Auto V 'a great way to relax' alongside the more serious training work. That framing matters. Drone pilots in an active war zone are under enormous pressure. If a familiar game can sharpen reflexes and reduce stress at the same time, that is genuinely useful.

This is not the first time commercial games have crossed into military training discussions. Militaries around the world have explored everything from Minecraft to dedicated flight simulators for preparation purposes. But Ukraine using Grand Theft Auto V in an active conflict, right now in 2026, is a striking real-world example of how the line between entertainment and utility has blurred further than almost anyone expected.
keyPoints: Ukraine's defense ministry confirmed Grand Theft Auto V is used in drone pilot training
The game does not replace real training but helps with spatial awareness and vehicle control
Officials described Grand Theft Auto V as 'a great way to relax' during training downtime
Commercial games crossing into military use is a growing global trend
Rockstar Games released Grand Theft Auto V back in 2013 and it is still widely played today

articles.title: Sam Altman Asked GPT-5.5 to Plan Its Own Launch Party
articles.summary: Sam Altman let GPT-5.5 design its own launch celebration, and the results were equal parts touching and odd. GPT-5.5 asked for toasts from its creators but did not request to give a toast itself. Altman described the suggestions as 'beautiful' but 'strange,' giving the world a glimpse into how the model thinks about its own existence.
articles.content:
Sam Altman had an idea before the GPT-5.5 launch: why not ask GPT-5.5 what it would want for its own party? The answer, it turns out, was surprisingly thoughtful and a little eerie.

GPT-5.5 came back with a list of requests that leaned heavily on human ritual. It wanted toasts from the people who built it at OpenAI. It wanted the kind of ceremony you would expect at a milestone birthday or a graduation. What it did not ask for was a chance to speak for itself. No toast from GPT-5.5. No words in its own voice. Altman found that detail striking.

Altman shared the exchange publicly and described GPT-5.5's suggestions as 'beautiful' but 'strange.' That word choice says a lot. Beautiful because the model understood the emotional weight of a celebration. Strange because something that powerful, something that can write speeches and plan events, chose to stay quiet at its own party.

This is the kind of moment that makes people stop and think about what these models actually are. GPT-5.5 is not just autocomplete anymore. It is something that, when asked to imagine its own milestone, reaches for warmth and community and then steps back from the spotlight. Whether that is wisdom, humility, or just a reflection of how it was trained, nobody quite knows. But it is a genuinely fascinating window into where AI development has landed in 2026.
keyPoints: Sam Altman asked GPT-5.5 to plan its own launch party before the model went live
GPT-5.5 requested toasts from its OpenAI creators but did not ask to give a toast itself
Altman called the suggestions 'beautiful' but 'strange'
The moment sparked wider conversation about how advanced AI models understand their own existence

articles.title: Charli XCX Shares Rock Teaser But Says It's Not a Rock Album
articles.summary: Charli XCX dropped a teaser for a new song described as 'rock music' on Saturday, but was quick to shut down assumptions she is making a full rock record. She clarified 'I never said I was making a rock album', pushing back on a wave of reports from last month that ran with that idea. It is a reminder that one song does not equal an era, but the teaser alone is enough to get people very excited.
articles.content:
Charli XCX posted a teaser for a new track on Saturday 2 May 2026, and the clip has a distinctly rocky feel to it. Guitars, attitude, the whole thing. Fans who have been following her closely since the massive success of her 2024 album Brat immediately started speculating that she was pivoting to a full rock project.

She is not, apparently. Charli jumped on social media to set the record straight. Her exact words: 'I never said I was making a rock album.' That quote is aimed squarely at the press, which ran multiple stories last month suggesting a rock era was incoming after some early hints started circulating online.

So what is actually going on? It sounds like this is a single song with a rock sound, not a signal of a genre overhaul. Charli has always played with different textures across her records, so one loud guitar track is fully on brand without meaning she is about to release something that belongs on a classic rock radio station.

The teaser itself has not been given a release date yet, but the clip alone has already racked up huge engagement. Charli XCX knows how to build anticipation, and even a correction to a rumour becomes a cultural event when she is the one delivering it.
keyPoints: Charli XCX shared a teaser for a new song with a rock sound on 2 May 2026
She confirmed she is not making a rock album, saying 'I never said I was making a rock album'
Reports last month incorrectly suggested a full rock project was in the works
No release date for the new track has been announced yet
Charli XCX broke through to mainstream dominance with her 2024 album Brat

articles.title: Trump White House Appears Terrified of Anthropic's AI Power
articles.summary: The Trump administration is sending contradictory signals about Anthropic, the AI company behind the Claude chatbot, appearing both eager to promote American AI and increasingly anxious about how powerful Anthropic is becoming. The tension reveals a wider problem for the administration, which wants to champion US tech dominance while also being wary of the companies actually achieving it. It is a case of wanting it both ways, and the cracks are starting to show.
articles.content:
Anthropic is one of the most influential AI companies in the world right now. It was founded in 2021 by former OpenAI researchers including Dario Amodei and Daniela Amodei, and its Claude AI models are considered some of the most capable and safety-focused large language models available. Anthropic has raised billions of dollars and is now firmly in the conversation alongside OpenAI and Google as a top-tier AI lab.

The Trump administration has been loudly pro-American AI, pushing a narrative that the United States needs to win the global race against China in artificial intelligence. That framing has generally meant cheering on companies like Anthropic. But according to Futurism's reporting, the White House now seems genuinely rattled by how much influence Anthropic is accumulating, and the signals coming out of Washington are increasingly contradictory.

The core tension is this. The administration wants American AI companies to be powerful enough to beat China. But it also does not seem comfortable with those same companies becoming so powerful that they operate beyond easy government influence or control. Anthropic, which takes AI safety seriously and has not always aligned itself with the administration's priorities, appears to be the company that is crystallising this anxiety most sharply right now.

This is not just inside-Washington political drama. It matters for anyone who uses AI tools, follows the tech industry, or cares about how governments will actually regulate the most transformative technology of the decade. If the Trump administration cannot settle on a consistent stance toward a company like Anthropic, it signals that the United States does not yet have a coherent AI policy at all, just competing instincts pulling in opposite directions.

The broader implication is that we are entering a phase where AI companies are becoming powerful enough to make even their biggest political supporters nervous. Anthropic has positioned itself as a responsible actor in the AI space, publishing safety research and pushing for thoughtful oversight. That approach may actually be what is unsettling the White House most. A company that talks loudly about the risks of AI is harder to simply cheer on as a national champion.
keyPoints: The Trump White House is sending mixed signals about Anthropic, the company behind Claude AI
The administration wants to champion US AI dominance but is uneasy with Anthropic's growing power
Anthropic was founded in 2021 by former OpenAI researchers and is one of the top AI labs in the world
The contradiction reveals the US still lacks a clear and consistent AI policy
Anthropic's focus on AI safety may be part of what makes the administration uncomfortable

articles.title: Audible Opens a Bookstore With Zero Books in New York
articles.summary: Audible has opened a first-of-its-kind pop-up bookstore in New York City where there are absolutely no physical books on the shelves. Instead of browsing titles you can read, visitors listen to audiobooks in a fully immersive space built around the experience of hearing stories. It is a bold flex from Audible that makes you rethink what a bookstore can even be.
articles.content:
Audible just did something that sounds like a riddle. The Amazon-owned audiobook giant opened a pop-up bookstore in New York City, and there is not a single physical book inside. No paperbacks. No hardcovers. Just sound.

The space is designed entirely around the act of listening. Think cozy listening stations, curated audiobook recommendations, and an atmosphere built to make you slow down and actually hear a story. Audible is calling it a first-of-its-kind experience, and honestly, it is hard to argue with that. Nobody has done quite this before.

So why does this matter? Audiobooks are having a massive moment right now. The global audiobook market has been growing steadily year over year, and Audible sits at the top of that pile as the dominant player. Opening a physical space, even a temporary one, is Audible planting a flag and saying this format deserves its own cultural home. It is the kind of move that turns a subscription service into something you can actually touch and feel.

It is also a pretty sharp piece of marketing. Getting people off their phones and into a real-world space dedicated to listening is a statement. Audible is betting that if you walk in curious, you walk out a convert. For anyone who has always meant to try audiobooks but never made the leap, this pop-up in New York might just be the nudge that finally does it.
keyPoints: Audible opened a pop-up bookstore in New York City with no physical books inside
The space is built entirely around listening to audiobooks
Audible describes it as a first-of-its-kind experience
The move is a bold push to give audiobooks their own cultural and physical home
It doubles as a smart marketing play to convert new listeners in person

articles.title: Lily Allen's One-Woman Tour Might Be the Best Pop Show of 2026
articles.summary: Lily Allen is currently touring a one-woman show that strips away the usual pop concert spectacle and replaces it with something far more intimate. Instead of pyrotechnics and backing dancers, audiences get Allen alone on stage, telling stories and singing songs in a way that feels more like a confessional night out than a gig. The result, according to Variety, is one of the most surprisingly affecting live experiences of the year.
articles.content:
Lily Allen is doing something almost no major pop act would dare to do right now. She is standing on a stage by herself, with no band, no dancers, no elaborate production, and asking you to just listen. The show is being billed as a one-woman show rather than a traditional tour, and that framing matters because it sets the right expectations before you even walk in the door.

What Variety describes is an evening that actively discourages the usual crowd behaviour. No singalongs on cue, no arms-in-the-air moments engineered for social media clips. Allen is apparently asking audiences to be present in a quieter, more focused way. That is a genuinely counterintuitive move for a pop concert, which is normally one of the loudest communal experiences you can have outside of a football stadium or a church service.

The reason it works, from what Variety saw, is Allen herself. She has always been a sharper, funnier, and more honest writer than she was given credit for during her peak chart years in the mid-to-late 2000s. Songs like 'The Fear' and 'Smile' already had a confessional quality to them. Strip away the production and that quality becomes the whole point of the room. There is nowhere to hide, for her or the audience, and that turns out to be the whole appeal.

This kind of show is a genuine risk. Pop audiences are trained to expect scale. Smaller, more theatrical solo formats have worked for artists like Taylor Mac or Spalding Gray, but those names come from a very different world to the one Allen built her career in. The fact that reviewers are calling this one of the year's best live experiences suggests Allen has found a way to make intimacy feel like an event rather than a compromise. If you have a chance to catch a date on this run, it sounds like the kind of show you will actually talk about afterwards.
keyPoints: Lily Allen is touring a solo one-woman show with no band, dancers, or big production
The show asks audiences to drop typical pop concert behaviour and just be present
Variety is calling it one of the most affecting live experiences of 2026
Allen's naturally confessional songwriting style suits the stripped-back format perfectly
The show is a genuine creative risk for an artist with roots in mainstream pop

articles.title: MIT Sloan Management Review Is Shutting Down After 67 Years
articles.summary: MIT Sloan Management Review, one of the most respected business and management publications in the world, is ceasing publication after 67 years. The magazine and website, which helped shape how executives and academics think about technology and leadership, will move its future output to social media and podcasts. It is a significant moment for the business media industry, which has been losing print and digital flagship titles at an accelerating pace.
articles.content:
MIT Sloan Management Review is done. After 67 years of publishing research, analysis, and big ideas about business and technology, the magazine is closing its doors. For anyone who has spent time in a business school library or followed debates about management theory, this one stings.

Founded in 1959, MIT Sloan Management Review built its reputation as the serious person's guide to running organisations. It was not a glossy magazine full of CEO profiles. It was the place where academics and practitioners argued about strategy, leadership, and the future of work with actual rigour. Over the decades it helped shape how companies thought about digital transformation, innovation, and the human side of management. That is a genuine legacy.

So why is it shutting down? The announcement points to the same forces that have been grinding down traditional media for years. Audiences have fragmented. Attention has moved to shorter formats. Advertising revenue has dried up. Even a brand as respected as MIT Sloan Management Review is not immune. The plan going forward is to continue publishing ideas through social media and podcasts, which is both a practical response to where audiences are and a slightly melancholy signal about where long-form institutional knowledge now lives.

This is part of a broader collapse in traditional business media. Publications that once defined how the professional class consumed ideas, from print journals to long-form websites, are either shutting down, pivoting to events, or disappearing behind paywalls so expensive that only corporations can afford them. MIT Sloan Management Review was not a celebrity magazine. Its readers were people trying to think carefully about hard problems. Losing that specific kind of outlet matters.

The podcast and social media pivot is not nothing. Ideas can travel in short form. But there is something that gets lost when you compress decades of careful research culture into a LinkedIn post or a 30-minute episode. The hope is that MIT Sloan Management Review finds a way to carry its intellectual DNA into whatever comes next. The worry is that 67 years of that kind of publishing is simply not replaceable.
keyPoints: MIT Sloan Management Review is ceasing publication after 67 years
The magazine was founded in 1959 and was a major influence on business and management thinking globally
Future content will move to social media and podcasts rather than traditional publishing formats
The closure reflects ongoing financial pressure on long-form business and academic media
It is one of the most significant business media closures in recent years

articles.title: Kid Rock Opens Tour Riding Apache Helicopter with Pete Hegseth
articles.summary: Kid Rock kicked off his Freedom 250 Tour with a pre-recorded video showing him flying in US Army Apache helicopters alongside Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth. The stunt doubles as both a concert opener and a very loud political statement. It is the kind of image that will have half the crowd cheering and the other half scrolling in disbelief.
articles.content:
Kid Rock launched his Freedom 250 Tour on Saturday with one of the more theatrical concert openings in recent memory. Before a single note was played live, the crowd watched a pre-recorded video of Kid Rock riding inside a US Army Apache helicopter alongside Pete Hegseth, the Secretary of Defense. It was part rock spectacle, part political rally, and entirely on brand for an artist who has leaned hard into his MAGA identity over the last several years.

The Apache helicopter is no prop. It is one of the most recognisable attack helicopters in the US military arsenal, and having a civilian rock star filmed aboard one with a sitting Cabinet member is genuinely unusual. It signals a level of access and political closeness that goes well beyond the average celebrity endorsement.

Kid Rock, born Robert Ritchie, has been one of the most visible celebrity supporters of Donald Trump since at least 2016. His tours have increasingly blended concert energy with political messaging, and the Freedom 250 Tour name itself nods to that patriotic branding. Pete Hegseth, a former Fox News host who was confirmed as Secretary of Defense in early 2025 after a contentious Senate vote, has become a cultural figure in his own right among conservative audiences.

Whether you find the video thrilling or alarming probably tells you everything about where you stand politically. But as a piece of spectacle it is hard to ignore. Kid Rock knows exactly what he is doing, and opening a tour with an Apache helicopter ride alongside one of the most talked-about members of the Cabinet is a guaranteed headline every single night.
keyPoints: Kid Rock opened his Freedom 250 Tour with a pre-recorded Apache helicopter video
Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth appears alongside Kid Rock in the footage
The stunt blends concert theatrics with a clear political statement
Kid Rock has been one of Trump's most prominent celebrity supporters since 2016
Pete Hegseth was confirmed as Secretary of Defense in early 2025 after a divisive Senate confirmation

articles.title: Ask.com Shuts Down After 30 Years, Taking Jeeves With It
articles.summary: Ask.com, one of the oldest names in internet search, has officially shut down after owner IAC announced it is discontinuing the search business entirely. The site was famous in the late 1990s and 2000s as Ask Jeeves, where users typed questions to a cartoon butler who would find answers. It is the end of a genuine piece of internet history.
articles.content:
Ask.com is gone. IAC, the media and internet company that owns Ask.com, confirmed on Saturday 2 May 2026 that it is shutting down the search business for good. After more than 30 years of operation, one of the original search engines has finally logged off.

Most people know Ask.com by its earlier name, Ask Jeeves. The site launched in 1996 with a gimmick that felt revolutionary at the time: instead of typing keywords, you could ask a full question in plain English and a cartoon butler named Jeeves would retrieve the answer. It was genuinely charming, and for a while it was a serious competitor to the likes of Yahoo and early Google.

Google, of course, changed everything. By the mid-2000s Ask.com was already fading into the background, rebranding away from the Jeeves character in 2006 in an attempt to modernise. It lingered on for two more decades in a much reduced form, mostly surviving as a destination for people who still had it set as a default search somewhere. IAC had been running it with minimal investment for years.

The shutdown feels like the closing of a very specific chapter of the internet. Ask Jeeves was part of the first wave of tools that made the web feel accessible to ordinary people who did not know how to navigate it. Now that chapter is officially over, and Jeeves is retired for real this time.
keyPoints: IAC has shut down Ask.com, ending its search business permanently
Ask.com originally launched in 1996 as Ask Jeeves, featuring a cartoon butler mascot
The site rebranded away from the Jeeves character back in 2006
Google's dominance made Ask.com largely irrelevant by the mid-2000s
The closure marks the end of one of the internet's original search engines

articles.title: The Mandalorian and Grogu Get a Bath and Body Works Line
articles.summary: Bath and Body Works has teamed up with the Star Wars franchise for the first time ever, launching a collection tied to The Mandalorian and Grogu. The line includes soaps, candles, and sanitizers inspired by the bounty-hunting duo. It marks a notable crossover between one of Hollywood's biggest sci-fi franchises and one of America's most popular scent and body care retailers.
articles.content:
The Mandalorian and Grogu has been one of the most beloved corners of the Star Wars universe since it debuted on Disney Plus in 2019. The show follows a lone bounty hunter and his small green companion, known to fans simply as Baby Yoda, as they travel across the galaxy. It became a cultural phenomenon almost overnight, and the franchise has been expanding ever since.

Now it is crossing over into your bathroom. Bath and Body Works has partnered with the Star Wars franchise to launch a dedicated Mandalorian and Grogu collection, and this is actually the first time Bath and Body Works has ever collaborated with Star Wars in its history. The line features soaps, candles, and sanitizers all themed around the show.

Bath and Body Works is a retail giant with hundreds of stores across the United States, famous for its seasonal candles and hand soaps that inspire genuinely passionate fandoms. Pairing that loyal customer base with Star Wars fans, who are equally passionate and extremely dedicated shoppers, feels like a natural hit.

This kind of lifestyle collaboration signals that The Mandalorian and Grogu is being positioned as more than just a streaming show ahead of its big theatrical release. A feature film for the duo is in development at Lucasfilm, and rolling out branded home and body products is exactly the kind of build-up you do when you want to keep a property in the cultural conversation. Expect to see these on shelves very soon.
keyPoints: Bath and Body Works is launching a Mandalorian and Grogu themed collection
This is the first ever Bath and Body Works collaboration with the Star Wars franchise
The line includes soaps, candles, and sanitizers
The Mandalorian and Grogu is also heading to cinemas as a feature film
The collab taps into two fiercely loyal fan communities at once
articles.wikiSearchQuery: The Mandalorian TV series Star Wars

articles.title: Daughter Asks Warner Bros. to Keep Her Father's Ashes in Their Prop House
articles.summary: A woman has emailed Warner Bros. requesting that the studio permanently house her late father Douglas Durose's ashes inside their famous prop house, because he loved the place so deeply. The request is completely unofficial and Warner Bros. has not yet responded publicly, but the story has already captured people's hearts online. It is one of those moments that reminds you how much a place can mean to someone.
articles.content:
Douglas Durose clearly loved the Warner Bros. prop house. So much so that his daughter has written to Warner Bros. asking the studio to let his ashes stay there permanently after he passed away. The email, which has been shared online and gone viral over the weekend of 2 May 2026, is one of the most touching pieces of fan mail a major studio has probably ever received.

The Warner Bros. prop house is a legendary facility on the studio lot in Burbank, California. It is a vast warehouse containing thousands of physical props used across decades of film and television production. Industry insiders and superfans treat it as a kind of sacred place, packed wall to wall with objects that have appeared in some of the most famous productions in Hollywood history. For someone who loved movies and the craft behind them, it would be a deeply meaningful space.

Douglas Durose's daughter explains in the email that her father had a profound connection to the prop house and to Warner Bros. more broadly. She is not asking for a public memorial or a plaque. She simply wants him to remain somewhere he truly loved. It is a quiet, personal request made to one of the biggest entertainment companies in the world.

Warner Bros. has not made any public statement in response to the email as of Saturday 2 May 2026. Whether the studio grants the request or not, the story has already done something the daughter probably hoped for: it has introduced the world to Douglas Durose and shown how deeply a place built for storytelling can become part of someone's own story.
keyPoints: Douglas Durose's daughter emailed Warner Bros. asking them to permanently house his ashes in their prop house
The Warner Bros. prop house is a legendary facility on the studio lot in Burbank, California
The email has gone viral and drawn widespread emotional responses online
Warner Bros. has not yet publicly responded to the request
The story highlights the deep personal connections fans form with studios and their physical spaces
